planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost in average to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; seeds benefit from a brief 2–4 week cold-moist stratification before sowing to improve germination rates. Site at woodland edges or along naturalistic fences where twining stems can climb through adjacent shrubs.

directions: Plant in average to moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade at a woodland edge or naturalistic fence where slender twining stems can climb through shrubs or simple supports. Carolina Milkvine is a softly hairy, heart-leaved native perennial in the milkweed family (Apocynaceae), producing small, star-shaped, dull brownish-purple flowers in summer that are followed by spindle-shaped seed pods splitting open to release seeds with silky white plumes. Like other milkweeds it exudes latex sap when cut and supports generalist milkweed bugs and potentially monarch butterflies as a larval host plant. It is an underused native vine well suited to naturalistic woodland garden settings where it weaves through existing shrubs without smothering them.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost in rocky, calcareous, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; cold-moist stratify seeds for 3–4 weeks before sowing to improve germination. Best sited in rock garden settings or along limestone-based slopes and canyon edges where drainage is excellent and competition from taller plants is limited.

directions: Plant in rocky, calcareous, lean, very well-drained soil in full sun to light shade; Limestone Milkvine is a native twining perennial vine of canyon edges, open rocky woodlands, and limestone outcrops in Texas and adjacent Oklahoma. It produces somewhat rough, heart-shaped leaves and small, dark maroon to brownish-purple, 5-petaled star-shaped flowers with a slight odor that attracts small flies as pollinators, followed by spindle-shaped seed pods that split to release wind-dispersed, plume-tailed seeds. Once established it is notably drought-tolerant and thrives without supplemental water in rocky, calcareous soils where most vines fail. It is an ecologically appropriate native vine for Texas Hill Country gardens seeking regionally adapted, low-water climbing plants.

planting.note: Direct sow at last frost in moist, open, well-drained soil in full sun; seeds can also be fall-sown to overwinter in the ground for natural cold stratification and earlier spring germination. Give plants a sturdy fence or trellis to climb — this is a vigorous grower and will quickly cover available support. Best suited to wildlife garden settings where free spreading is acceptable.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun along a fence, trellis, or through a sturdy shrub; Climbing Milkweed (also called Honeyvine) is a vigorous twining native perennial in the milkweed subfamily, producing smooth, heart-shaped leaves and dense clusters of small, sweetly honey-fragrant white flowers from July through September that are outstanding nectar sources for monarch butterflies and many native bees. It is native to moist, open, disturbed ground, fencerows, and thicket edges throughout the eastern and central US, and produces classic milkweed seed pods that split to release plume-tailed seeds. Climbing Milkweed can spread aggressively from both seed and rhizomes in favorable conditions and is best suited to naturalistic or wildlife garden settings where its habit of covering fences and scrambling through shrubs is welcome rather than a problem.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm in very well-drained, dry, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; in zones 9–11 plants may be started in late winter for earlier establishment. Avoid amended or rich soils — Fringed Twining Milkweed performs best in lean, xeric conditions that mimic its native desert habitat. Provide a low trellis or nearby shrub for the twining stems to climb.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry, sandy or rocky, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; Fringed Twining Milkweed is a drought-adapted native perennial milkweed vine of desert shrubland, washes, and roadsides from California to Texas in the southwestern US. It produces long, narrow to linear leaves and clusters of small, pale to rose-pink, star-shaped flowers with a distinctive fringed corona that bloom across a long summer season. Like true milkweeds it produces latex sap and classic spindle-shaped seed pods with plume-tailed, wind-dispersed seeds. It is a particularly important late-season milkweed resource for monarch butterflies on their fall migration routes through the Southwest, and should be kept dry and given excellent drainage to perform well — it declines quickly in heavy or poorly drained soils.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very dry, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun for natural winter cold stratification and spring germination; in mild desert climates (zones 8–10) seeds can also be sown in early winter. Do not amend or fertilize — Longbeak Streptanthella thrives in the same impoverished desert soils where most plants fail. Scatter seed thinly across bare or sparsely vegetated ground and press lightly into contact with soil surface.

directions: Direct sow in fall in lean, very dry, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun for spring germination and bloom; Longbeak Streptanthella is a slender native annual in the mustard family (Brassicaceae) found on dry, sandy or gravelly desert flats and open creosote scrub from California to Texas. It produces very narrow, clasping stem leaves and elongated racemes of small, pale yellow to white, 4-petaled mustard flowers in spring that are followed by the distinctive, very long and narrow, strongly curved seed pods that give the plant its common name. It is one of many early-blooming native annual mustards that can carpet desert flats in favorable wet years, providing important early nectar for native bees and small butterflies emerging in spring. It is entirely self-sufficient once established in appropriate lean desert soils and requires no irrigation, fertilization, or maintenance.
